title: Haemophilia A
oneLiner: X-linked recessive bleeding disorder due to factor VIII deficiency; presents with spontaneous hemarthroses, easy bruising, and a prolonged aPTT that corrects with mixing.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic Vignette:**  
A 2-year-old boy is brought to the Emergency Department with a warm, swollen, and tender left knee. His mother reports he started limping after a minor fall from standing yesterday; he now refuses to bear weight. On examination, the knee is held in slight flexion with marked limitation of movement. He has several bruises on his shins and arms, and his immunisation records show he was not given the MMR vaccine on schedule because of a family history of "bleeding problems." His paternal uncle died of a bleed after a dental extraction.

**Dissecting the Vignette:**

- **Sex (male) and age (toddler)** – X-linked recessive disorders (like haemophilia A) classically affect males, with onset when ambulation and minor trauma begin.
- **Spontaneous/poorly provoked haemarthrosis** – a hallmark of severe haemophilia; the knee joint is the most common site (synovial tissue lacks tissue factor, so bleeding from minor trauma continues unchecked).
- **Warmth and swelling confined to the joint** – differentiates from septic arthritis (which would have high fever, systemically unwell) or a fracture (bony tenderness, deformity).
- **Family history of bleeding in maternal uncle** – X-linked inheritance pattern: the mother is a carrier.
- **Bruises at multiple sites** – suggests impaired clotting not just from trauma but from minor everyday bumps.
- **Refusal to bear weight** – pain and guarding; the child may have a "pseudoparalysis" of the limb mimicking fracture or septic arthritis.

**Atypical Presentations That May Trick You:**

- **Intracranial haemorrhage in a neonate** – prolonged after a traumatic delivery, no visible bruises, but unexplained irritability, vomiting, or seizures. Diagnosis delayed until CT shows subdural or parenchymal bleed.
- **Post-circumcision bleeding** – the first presentation of mild haemophilia A in an otherwise well infant. Circumcision is a haemostatic challenge; if undiagnosed, bleeding may be severe enough to require transfusion.
- **Adult male with new-onset bleeding** – consider acquired haemophilia A (autoimmune inhibitor to FVIII). Presents with extensive skin ecchymoses, soft tissue haematomas, or prolonged bleeding from trivial wounds – but importantly, *no past history of bleeding* and *no family history*. This patient is often >60 years old, postpartum, or has an underlying autoimmune disease.

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-Language Definition:**  
Haemophilia A is a congenital bleeding disorder caused by deficiency of clotting factor VIII (FVIII). It is inherited in an X-linked recessive manner, so almost all affected individuals are male. Severity correlates directly with residual FVIII activity: severe disease (<1% of normal) leads to spontaneous bleeding into joints and muscles; moderate (1-5%) causes bleeding after moderate trauma; mild (5-40%) may only bleed after major surgery or injury.

**Why It Matters Clinically:**

- **Frequency:** Haemophilia A is the most common severe congenital bleeding disorder, affecting ~1 in 5,000 live male births worldwide.
- **Stakes:** Untreated severe disease results in crippling, painful haemophilic arthropathy by adolescence, intracranial haemorrhage (a leading cause of death), and lifelong morbidity. With modern prophylactic therapy, quality of life and life expectancy approach that of the general population.
- **Common Pitfalls:** (1) Misdiagnosing a swollen joint in a boy as septic arthritis – doing an arthrocentesis in an undiagnosed haemophiliac can cause catastrophic bleeding. (2) Assuming prolonged aPTT always means haemophilia without ordering a mixing study – an inhibitor (acquired haemophilia) will not correct. (3) Forgetting to ask about family history – 30% of cases are new mutations, so its absence does not rule out haemophilia.

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The Story:**  
FVIII is a critical cofactor in the intrinsic coagulation cascade. It sits on the X chromosome (Xq28). When a mutation (most commonly the intron 22 inversion) disrupts its production, FVIII levels fall. In the intrinsic pathway, activated factor IX (FIXa) needs FVIII as a cofactor to activate factor X (FX). Without sufficient FVIII, the rate of FX activation drops dramatically – think of it as an enzyme reaction where the cofactor is the limiting reagent. The extrinsic pathway (tissue factor / factor VII) is intact, so the PT is normal. But the intrinsic pathway is crippled, leading to a prolonged aPTT.

**Why Each Sign Happens (Mechanism → Sign):**

- **Spontaneous haemarthrosis** – Synovial lining is rich in capillaries but low in tissue factor; after even trivial microtrauma, the deficiency means clots form slowly and are unstable. Blood pours into the joint space. The synovium does not "turn on" the extrinsic pathway like deeper tissues. Over time, iron from the haemosiderin in the joint triggers a chronic inflammatory synovitis – that's why recurrent bleeds beget more bleeds.
- **Easy bruising** – Small vessel injuries that would normally be sealed by a stable fibrin plug continue to ooze because the intrinsic amplification loop is broken. The bruises are often large and deep, not just superficial petechiae.
- **Deep muscle haematomas (iliopsoas, forearm, calf)** – Muscle compartments after a contusion bleed briskly; without adequate clot retraction and stabilization, the haematoma expands, may compromise nerves (fernoral nerve palsy in iliopsoas bleed) and cause compartment syndrome.
- **Prolonged aPTT** – Standard laboratory aPTT reagents activate the intrinsic pathway. With low FVIII, the time to clot is prolonged. The addition of normal plasma (mixing study) supplies FVIII and fully corrects the aPTT – this distinguishes a factor deficiency from an inhibitor (which would not correct).

**Connecting Pathophysiology to Pattern Recognition:**

- The **young male toddler** with a knee bleed is the classic presentation of severe disease. The joint most often involved is weight-bearing (knee, ankle, elbow). Over 90% of bleeds in severe haemophilia occur in joints. The pattern of recurrent haemarthroses → synovitis → contracture → arthropathy is the natural history if left untreated.
- The **newborn with intracranial bleed** – delivery trauma disrupts bridging veins; low FVIII prevents durable clot formation. This can happen even in mild haemophilia if the delivery was instrumental.
- The **adult with acquired haemophilia** – here, autoantibodies bind to the active site of FVIII, blocking function. But because the inhibitor is an antibody (usually IgG), it neutralises FVIII both in the patient and in the mixing study plasma. Hence the aPTT remains prolonged even after mixing. Bleeding is into soft tissues (skin, muscle, retroperitoneum) – rarely into joints. This is a critical pattern-recognition cue.

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem Clue to Identify |
|----------|-------|------------------------|
| **Genetic (inherited) – most common** | X-linked recessive deficiency of FVIII (F8 gene mutations, e.g. intron 22 inversion, missense, nonsense) | "Young boy with knee bleed; maternal uncle bled after tooth extraction" |
| **Acquired (autoantibody inhibitor)** | Autoimmune – postpartum, elderly, drug-induced (e.g., penicillin, phenytoin), systemic lupus erythematosus, haematological malignancy | "Elderly woman with new-onset easy bruising; aPTT prolonged and does NOT correct with mixing; no family history" |
| **Iatrogenic (sporadic case from defective product)** | Historically, contaminated factor concentrates (HIV, hepatitis C) – nowadays with recombinant factors, rarely an issue | "Patient with haemophilia A who developed HIV in 1980s" |
| **Idiopathic (new mutation)** | Approximately 30% of cases have *de novo* mutation in germline, no family history | "Sporadic case, no affected relatives, but severe disease from birth" |

**Most Common Cause:** Inherited X-linked recessive FVIII deficiency (>99% of cases in children). Adults with a new diagnosis are more likely to have an acquired inhibitor.

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic Criteria:**  
The diagnosis is based on **low FVIII activity** in the presence of **normal VWF** and a **normal PT**. Severity is graded by FVIII activity:
- Severe: <1% (spontaneous bleeding)
- Moderate: 1–5% (bleeding after minor trauma)
- Mild: >5% to <40% (bleeding only after major or surgical trauma)

**Coagulation Screen (First Step):**

| Test | What It Shows | Why You Order It | How to Interpret |
|------|---------------|------------------|------------------|
| Prothrombin Time (PT) | Normal | Screens extrinsic pathway – should be normal in pure factor VIII deficiency | Normal PT rules out factor VII deficiency, liver disease, warfarin effect |
| Activated Partial Thromboplastin Time (aPTT) | **Prolonged** | Screens intrinsic pathway (factors VIII, IX, XI, XII) | Prolonged aPTT with normal PT points to deficiency of intrinsic factors |
| aPTT Mixing Study (immediate & after 1-hour incubation) | **Corrects** (immediately) when normal plasma is added | Distinguishes factor deficiency from inhibitor | If immediate correction then → factor deficiency. If does not correct → likely inhibitor (acquired hemophilia) |
| FVIII Activity Assay (one-stage clot-based) | **Low** | Confirms and quantifies FVIII deficiency | Severity based on % activity (see above) |
| von Willebrand Factor Antigen (VWF:Ag) & Ristocetin Cofactor Activity | **Normal** | Rules out von Willebrand disease (which also causes low FVIII because VWF carries FVIII) | If VWF:Ag is low, the low FVIII is secondary to vWD (not hemophilia A) |
| Bethesda Assay (if inhibitor suspected) | **Inhibitor titre** (Bethesda units) | Quantifies FVIII antibody if mixing study fails to correct | >0.5 Bethesda Units indicates inhibitor (acquired hemophilia) |

**Pattern-Recognition Cue – Single Finding That Confirms / Rules Out:**

- **Confirming:** Isolated low FVIII activity (<40%) with normal VWF, normal PT, and a mixing study that corrects is diagnostic of haemophilia A.
- **Ruling out:** Normal aPTT effectively excludes clinically significant haemophilia A (though some mild forms may have borderline aPTT; FVIII assay is definitive).

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (First Hour of a Major Bleed):**

| Situation | Management | Reasoning |
|------------|------------|-----------|
| Acute haemarthrosis or muscle bleed | **Factor VIII concentrate** 50 IU/kg intravenously (for mild/moderate bleed) or 100 IU/kg (for life/limb-threatening bleed). Followed by maintenance 25 IU/kg every 8-12 hours for 24-48 hours. | Replace the missing factor to achieve haemostasis. Each IU/kg raises plasma FVIII by ~2%. Aim for 80-100% initial activity. Repeat boluses maintain trough >30-50%. |
| Mild haemophilia A (known FVIII >5%) | **Desmopressin (DDAVP)** 0.3 mcg/kg IV (or SC) [or intranasal 300 mcg for older children/adults] – but only if pre-test showed FVIII response (give trial dose) | Releases VWF from endothelium, which carries bound FVIII, raising FVIII 2-5 fold. Effective only if baseline FVIII >5% and no inhibitor. |
| Adjunct for mucosal bleeding | **Tranexamic acid** 1 g IV (or 25 mg/kg PO) loading, then 1 g TDS | Inhibits fibrinolysis; especially useful in dental bleeding, epistaxis, menorrhagia. Do NOT use in haematuria (clot colic). |
| Pain control | **Paracetamol** (acetaminophen) +/- short-course opioids (e.g., codeine 1 mg/kg PO). Avoid NSAIDs | NSAIDs (aspirin, ibuprofen) impair platelet function and worsen bleeding – **NEVER use in haemophilia** unless under strict haematology guidance for synovitis. |

**Ward Management (24–72 Hours):**

- **Factor monitoring:** Measure FVIII trough level every 12 hours; adjust dose to maintain >30-50% for major bleeds, >10-20% for minor. Continue for 5-7 days for severe bleeds.
- **Splinting and physiotherapy:** Immobilise the joint for 2-3 days, then gentle passive range-of-motion. Early mobilisation reduces contractures. Avoid full weight-bearing until pain and swelling resolved.
- **Avoid IM injections** – they can cause painful intramuscular haematomas. Use oral/IV routes.
- **Immunisations:** Give MMR, varicella as usual (live vaccines safe if on factor prophylaxis). Hepatitis B vaccine recommended (haemophilia patients who receive plasma-derived products were historically at risk).

**Long-Term Management:**

| What | Why |
|------|-----|
| **Prophylaxis – standard of care in severe haemophilia** | Factor VIII 25-40 IU/kg intravenously 3 times per week (or every other day) in severe disease. Goal: maintain trough FVIII >1% to prevent spontaneous bleeds and preserve joint function. Started at age 1-2 before first joint bleed. |
| **Intermediate vs. High Dose** | Intermediate: 15-25 IU/kg 3x/week. High: 25-40 IU/kg 3x/week (trough >1%). High better prevents joint damage. |
| **Extended half-life products** | E.g., efmoroctocog alfa (Pegylated FVIII) – 50 IU/kg every 5 days. Reduces injection frequency. |
| **Immune tolerance induction (ITI)** | If inhibitor develops (antibodies neutralise FVIII), high-dose daily FVIII (100 IU/kg/day) for up to 18 months eradicates the inhibitor. Alternative: bypassing agents for acute bleeds (rFVIIa 90 mcg/kg or aPCC 50 U/kg). |
| **Avoidance of high-risk activities** | Advise against contact sports (rugby, boxing). Encourage swimming, cycling, golf (with knee/elbow pads). |

**What NOT to Do (Common Pitfalls):**

- Do not give DDAVP to a patient with severe haemophilia (FVIII <1%) – it will not raise FVIII enough to stop bleeding.
- Do not perform a lumbar puncture or joint aspiration without factor cover (target FVIII >50% for safe procedure).
- Do not prescribe NSAIDs (including topical) for pain – risk of catastrophic bleed.
- Do not assume a baby is unaffected because of no family history – check cord blood FVIII in males from known carriers, or do genetic testing.
- Do not forget to check FVIII trough levels before discharge – a low trough predicts rebleeding.

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | **Haemophilia A** | **Haemophilia B (Christmas Disease)** | **von Willebrand Disease (vWD)** | **Acquired Haemophilia A** |
|---------|-------------------|---------------------------------------|----------------------------------|-----------------------------|
| **Inheritance** | X-linked recessive | X-linked recessive | Autosomal dominant (most) | Acquired (autoimmune) |
| **Deficiency** | Factor VIII | Factor IX | von Willebrand factor (with secondary FVIII drop) | Autoantibody to Factor VIII |
| **Bleeding Pattern** | Deep joint bleeds (knee, ankle, elbow), muscle haematomas | Same as Haemophilia A | Mucocutaneous (epistaxis, menorrhagia, gum bleeding), but can have post-surgical bleeding; rarely haemarthrosis | Skin ecchymoses, soft tissue haematomas, retroperitoneal and intracranial bleeds; **joint bleeds are rare** |
| **Sex** | Almost always male | Almost always male | Male and female equally | Male or female; often older adults or postpartum |
| **Family History** | Often positive (maternal uncle, brother) | Often positive | Often positive (autosomal) | **Negative** – new onset in adult |
| **Screening Labs** | aPTT ↑, PT normal, mixing corrects | aPTT ↑, PT normal, mixing corrects | aPTT ↑ (if FVIII low), PT normal, bleeding time ↑, PFA-100 ↑ | aPTT ↑↑, mixing **does not correct** |
| **Specific Lab Confirmation** | FVIII activity ↓, VWF:Ag normal | FIX activity ↓ | VWF:Ag ↓, ristocetin cofactor activity ↓, FVIII activity may be ↓ | Bethesda assay positive, FVIII activity ↓, mixing fails |
| **Key Differentiator** | Low FVIII with normal VWF – pattern of joint bleeds | Low FIX – same pattern but factor IX (heparin-binding) | Mucocutaneous bleeding + low VWF; bleeding time prolonged | No family history, adult, **mixing study fails**, bleeding in soft tissues not joints |

**Pattern cue:** If a child presents with a haemarthrosis and prolonged aPTT, the two X-linked disorders (A and B) are top. Differentiate by specific factor assays. If an adult presents with new-onset easy bruising and aPTT not correcting, think acquired haemophilia A.

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | How It Presents | Key Signs | What To Do |
|--------------|----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Chronic Haemophilic Arthropathy** | Repeated haemarthroses → synovitis → cartilage loss → joint contractures and ankylosis. Pain, decreased range of motion, deformity. | Warm, boggy knee; flexion contracture; MRI shows synovial hypertrophy and bone erosions. | Early prophylaxis prevents it. Established arthropathy: physiotherapy, replacement joints (tolerated well with factor cover). |
| **Intracranial Haemorrhage** | Headache, vomiting, altered consciousness, focal neurological deficit, seizures. Often after minor head trauma. | GCS drop, papilloedema, unilateral weakness. CT shows subdural, intraparenchymal, or subarachnoid blood. | **Emergency:** Administer FVIII 100 IU/kg IV stat, call haematology, neurosurgeon, ICU. Repeat factor to maintain trough >80% for 7-10 days. |
| **Iliopsoas Bleed (Compartment Syndrome)** | Groin or lower back pain, hip held in flexion, unable to extend thigh. Femoral nerve compression → anterior thigh numbness, quadriceps weakness. | Positive psoas sign (pain on hip extension), reduced sensation over anterior thigh, weak knee extension. | FVIII 50 IU/kg, bedrest, avoid flexed position. If compartment syndrome confirmed (compartment pressure >30 mmHg), urgent fasciotomy. |
| **Inhibitor Development** | Patient's bleeds no longer respond to standard FVIII doses; aPTT remains prolonged despite FVIII infusion. | Mixing study fails to correct, Bethesda titre >0.5. | For acute bleeds: bypassing agents (rFVIIa 90 mcg/kg every 2-3 hours; or aPCC 50 U/kg 12-hourly). Eradicate inhibitor with ITI. |
| **Transfusion-Transmitted Infections (historical)** | HCV (hepatitis C), HIV (now rare with recombinant products). | Abnormal LFTs, jaundice, HIV seroconversion. | Screen all haemophilia patients for HCV and HIV who received plasma-derived concentrates before 1990s. |

**Late-Presentation Pattern (What If Untreated/Unrecognised?):**
- A 10-year-old boy from a region with limited access to healthcare presents with bilateral knee contractures, severe pain, inability to walk. He has had multiple transient knee swellings since toddlerhood, each treated with rest and/or folk remedies. Family history: several maternal uncles died young from "bleeding fits." This is end-stage haemophilic arthropathy – irreversible joint destruction. Lifesaving intervention: factor replacement, orthopaedic referral for joint replacement, and lifelong prophylaxis to prevent further bleeds.

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonic: "HAEMOPHILIA"**  
- **H** – Hemarthrosis (joint bleeding – hallmark)  
- **A** – aPTT prolonged  
- **E** – X-linked (E for "X")  
- **M** – Mixing study corrects (factor deficiency)  
- **O

rawMarkdown:
## Haemophilia A
**One-liner:** X-linked recessive bleeding disorder due to factor VIII deficiency; presents with spontaneous hemarthroses, easy bruising, and a prolonged aPTT that corrects with mixing.

---

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic Vignette:**  
A 2-year-old boy is brought to the Emergency Department with a warm, swollen, and tender left knee. His mother reports he started limping after a minor fall from standing yesterday; he now refuses to bear weight. On examination, the knee is held in slight flexion with marked limitation of movement. He has several bruises on his shins and arms, and his immunisation records show he was not given the MMR vaccine on schedule because of a family history of "bleeding problems." His paternal uncle died of a bleed after a dental extraction.

**Dissecting the Vignette:**

- **Sex (male) and age (toddler)** – X-linked recessive disorders (like haemophilia A) classically affect males, with onset when ambulation and minor trauma begin.
- **Spontaneous/poorly provoked haemarthrosis** – a hallmark of severe haemophilia; the knee joint is the most common site (synovial tissue lacks tissue factor, so bleeding from minor trauma continues unchecked).
- **Warmth and swelling confined to the joint** – differentiates from septic arthritis (which would have high fever, systemically unwell) or a fracture (bony tenderness, deformity).
- **Family history of bleeding in maternal uncle** – X-linked inheritance pattern: the mother is a carrier.
- **Bruises at multiple sites** – suggests impaired clotting not just from trauma but from minor everyday bumps.
- **Refusal to bear weight** – pain and guarding; the child may have a "pseudoparalysis" of the limb mimicking fracture or septic arthritis.

**Atypical Presentations That May Trick You:**

- **Intracranial haemorrhage in a neonate** – prolonged after a traumatic delivery, no visible bruises, but unexplained irritability, vomiting, or seizures. Diagnosis delayed until CT shows subdural or parenchymal bleed.
- **Post-circumcision bleeding** – the first presentation of mild haemophilia A in an otherwise well infant. Circumcision is a haemostatic challenge; if undiagnosed, bleeding may be severe enough to require transfusion.
- **Adult male with new-onset bleeding** – consider acquired haemophilia A (autoimmune inhibitor to FVIII). Presents with extensive skin ecchymoses, soft tissue haematomas, or prolonged bleeding from trivial wounds – but importantly, *no past history of bleeding* and *no family history*. This patient is often >60 years old, postpartum, or has an underlying autoimmune disease.

---

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-Language Definition:**  
Haemophilia A is a congenital bleeding disorder caused by deficiency of clotting factor VIII (FVIII). It is inherited in an X-linked recessive manner, so almost all affected individuals are male. Severity correlates directly with residual FVIII activity: severe disease (<1% of normal) leads to spontaneous bleeding into joints and muscles; moderate (1-5%) causes bleeding after moderate trauma; mild (5-40%) may only bleed after major surgery or injury.

**Why It Matters Clinically:**

- **Frequency:** Haemophilia A is the most common severe congenital bleeding disorder, affecting ~1 in 5,000 live male births worldwide.
- **Stakes:** Untreated severe disease results in crippling, painful haemophilic arthropathy by adolescence, intracranial haemorrhage (a leading cause of death), and lifelong morbidity. With modern prophylactic therapy, quality of life and life expectancy approach that of the general population.
- **Common Pitfalls:** (1) Misdiagnosing a swollen joint in a boy as septic arthritis – doing an arthrocentesis in an undiagnosed haemophiliac can cause catastrophic bleeding. (2) Assuming prolonged aPTT always means haemophilia without ordering a mixing study – an inhibitor (acquired haemophilia) will not correct. (3) Forgetting to ask about family history – 30% of cases are new mutations, so its absence does not rule out haemophilia.

---

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The Story:**  
FVIII is a critical cofactor in the intrinsic coagulation cascade. It sits on the X chromosome (Xq28). When a mutation (most commonly the intron 22 inversion) disrupts its production, FVIII levels fall. In the intrinsic pathway, activated factor IX (FIXa) needs FVIII as a cofactor to activate factor X (FX). Without sufficient FVIII, the rate of FX activation drops dramatically – think of it as an enzyme reaction where the cofactor is the limiting reagent. The extrinsic pathway (tissue factor / factor VII) is intact, so the PT is normal. But the intrinsic pathway is crippled, leading to a prolonged aPTT.

**Why Each Sign Happens (Mechanism → Sign):**

- **Spontaneous haemarthrosis** – Synovial lining is rich in capillaries but low in tissue factor; after even trivial microtrauma, the deficiency means clots form slowly and are unstable. Blood pours into the joint space. The synovium does not "turn on" the extrinsic pathway like deeper tissues. Over time, iron from the haemosiderin in the joint triggers a chronic inflammatory synovitis – that's why recurrent bleeds beget more bleeds.
- **Easy bruising** – Small vessel injuries that would normally be sealed by a stable fibrin plug continue to ooze because the intrinsic amplification loop is broken. The bruises are often large and deep, not just superficial petechiae.
- **Deep muscle haematomas (iliopsoas, forearm, calf)** – Muscle compartments after a contusion bleed briskly; without adequate clot retraction and stabilization, the haematoma expands, may compromise nerves (fernoral nerve palsy in iliopsoas bleed) and cause compartment syndrome.
- **Prolonged aPTT** – Standard laboratory aPTT reagents activate the intrinsic pathway. With low FVIII, the time to clot is prolonged. The addition of normal plasma (mixing study) supplies FVIII and fully corrects the aPTT – this distinguishes a factor deficiency from an inhibitor (which would not correct).

**Connecting Pathophysiology to Pattern Recognition:**

- The **young male toddler** with a knee bleed is the classic presentation of severe disease. The joint most often involved is weight-bearing (knee, ankle, elbow). Over 90% of bleeds in severe haemophilia occur in joints. The pattern of recurrent haemarthroses → synovitis → contracture → arthropathy is the natural history if left untreated.
- The **newborn with intracranial bleed** – delivery trauma disrupts bridging veins; low FVIII prevents durable clot formation. This can happen even in mild haemophilia if the delivery was instrumental.
- The **adult with acquired haemophilia** – here, autoantibodies bind to the active site of FVIII, blocking function. But because the inhibitor is an antibody (usually IgG), it neutralises FVIII both in the patient and in the mixing study plasma. Hence the aPTT remains prolonged even after mixing. Bleeding is into soft tissues (skin, muscle, retroperitoneum) – rarely into joints. This is a critical pattern-recognition cue.

---

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Cause | Stem Clue to Identify |
|----------|-------|------------------------|
| **Genetic (inherited) – most common** | X-linked recessive deficiency of FVIII (F8 gene mutations, e.g. intron 22 inversion, missense, nonsense) | "Young boy with knee bleed; maternal uncle bled after tooth extraction" |
| **Acquired (autoantibody inhibitor)** | Autoimmune – postpartum, elderly, drug-induced (e.g., penicillin, phenytoin), systemic lupus erythematosus, haematological malignancy | "Elderly woman with new-onset easy bruising; aPTT prolonged and does NOT correct with mixing; no family history" |
| **Iatrogenic (sporadic case from defective product)** | Historically, contaminated factor concentrates (HIV, hepatitis C) – nowadays with recombinant factors, rarely an issue | "Patient with haemophilia A who developed HIV in 1980s" |
| **Idiopathic (new mutation)** | Approximately 30% of cases have *de novo* mutation in germline, no family history | "Sporadic case, no affected relatives, but severe disease from birth" |

**Most Common Cause:** Inherited X-linked recessive FVIII deficiency (>99% of cases in children). Adults with a new diagnosis are more likely to have an acquired inhibitor.

---

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic Criteria:**  
The diagnosis is based on **low FVIII activity** in the presence of **normal VWF** and a **normal PT**. Severity is graded by FVIII activity:
- Severe: <1% (spontaneous bleeding)
- Moderate: 1–5% (bleeding after minor trauma)
- Mild: >5% to <40% (bleeding only after major or surgical trauma)

**Coagulation Screen (First Step):**

| Test | What It Shows | Why You Order It | How to Interpret |
|------|---------------|------------------|------------------|
| Prothrombin Time (PT) | Normal | Screens extrinsic pathway – should be normal in pure factor VIII deficiency | Normal PT rules out factor VII deficiency, liver disease, warfarin effect |
| Activated Partial Thromboplastin Time (aPTT) | **Prolonged** | Screens intrinsic pathway (factors VIII, IX, XI, XII) | Prolonged aPTT with normal PT points to deficiency of intrinsic factors |
| aPTT Mixing Study (immediate & after 1-hour incubation) | **Corrects** (immediately) when normal plasma is added | Distinguishes factor deficiency from inhibitor | If immediate correction then → factor deficiency. If does not correct → likely inhibitor (acquired hemophilia) |
| FVIII Activity Assay (one-stage clot-based) | **Low** | Confirms and quantifies FVIII deficiency | Severity based on % activity (see above) |
| von Willebrand Factor Antigen (VWF:Ag) & Ristocetin Cofactor Activity | **Normal** | Rules out von Willebrand disease (which also causes low FVIII because VWF carries FVIII) | If VWF:Ag is low, the low FVIII is secondary to vWD (not hemophilia A) |
| Bethesda Assay (if inhibitor suspected) | **Inhibitor titre** (Bethesda units) | Quantifies FVIII antibody if mixing study fails to correct | >0.5 Bethesda Units indicates inhibitor (acquired hemophilia) |

**Pattern-Recognition Cue – Single Finding That Confirms / Rules Out:**

- **Confirming:** Isolated low FVIII activity (<40%) with normal VWF, normal PT, and a mixing study that corrects is diagnostic of haemophilia A.
- **Ruling out:** Normal aPTT effectively excludes clinically significant haemophilia A (though some mild forms may have borderline aPTT; FVIII assay is definitive).

---

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (First Hour of a Major Bleed):**

| Situation | Management | Reasoning |
|------------|------------|-----------|
| Acute haemarthrosis or muscle bleed | **Factor VIII concentrate** 50 IU/kg intravenously (for mild/moderate bleed) or 100 IU/kg (for life/limb-threatening bleed). Followed by maintenance 25 IU/kg every 8-12 hours for 24-48 hours. | Replace the missing factor to achieve haemostasis. Each IU/kg raises plasma FVIII by ~2%. Aim for 80-100% initial activity. Repeat boluses maintain trough >30-50%. |
| Mild haemophilia A (known FVIII >5%) | **Desmopressin (DDAVP)** 0.3 mcg/kg IV (or SC) [or intranasal 300 mcg for older children/adults] – but only if pre-test showed FVIII response (give trial dose) | Releases VWF from endothelium, which carries bound FVIII, raising FVIII 2-5 fold. Effective only if baseline FVIII >5% and no inhibitor. |
| Adjunct for mucosal bleeding | **Tranexamic acid** 1 g IV (or 25 mg/kg PO) loading, then 1 g TDS | Inhibits fibrinolysis; especially useful in dental bleeding, epistaxis, menorrhagia. Do NOT use in haematuria (clot colic). |
| Pain control | **Paracetamol** (acetaminophen) +/- short-course opioids (e.g., codeine 1 mg/kg PO). Avoid NSAIDs | NSAIDs (aspirin, ibuprofen) impair platelet function and worsen bleeding – **NEVER use in haemophilia** unless under strict haematology guidance for synovitis. |

**Ward Management (24–72 Hours):**

- **Factor monitoring:** Measure FVIII trough level every 12 hours; adjust dose to maintain >30-50% for major bleeds, >10-20% for minor. Continue for 5-7 days for severe bleeds.
- **Splinting and physiotherapy:** Immobilise the joint for 2-3 days, then gentle passive range-of-motion. Early mobilisation reduces contractures. Avoid full weight-bearing until pain and swelling resolved.
- **Avoid IM injections** – they can cause painful intramuscular haematomas. Use oral/IV routes.
- **Immunisations:** Give MMR, varicella as usual (live vaccines safe if on factor prophylaxis). Hepatitis B vaccine recommended (haemophilia patients who receive plasma-derived products were historically at risk).

**Long-Term Management:**

| What | Why |
|------|-----|
| **Prophylaxis – standard of care in severe haemophilia** | Factor VIII 25-40 IU/kg intravenously 3 times per week (or every other day) in severe disease. Goal: maintain trough FVIII >1% to prevent spontaneous bleeds and preserve joint function. Started at age 1-2 before first joint bleed. |
| **Intermediate vs. High Dose** | Intermediate: 15-25 IU/kg 3x/week. High: 25-40 IU/kg 3x/week (trough >1%). High better prevents joint damage. |
| **Extended half-life products** | E.g., efmoroctocog alfa (Pegylated FVIII) – 50 IU/kg every 5 days. Reduces injection frequency. |
| **Immune tolerance induction (ITI)** | If inhibitor develops (antibodies neutralise FVIII), high-dose daily FVIII (100 IU/kg/day) for up to 18 months eradicates the inhibitor. Alternative: bypassing agents for acute bleeds (rFVIIa 90 mcg/kg or aPCC 50 U/kg). |
| **Avoidance of high-risk activities** | Advise against contact sports (rugby, boxing). Encourage swimming, cycling, golf (with knee/elbow pads). |

**What NOT to Do (Common Pitfalls):**

- Do not give DDAVP to a patient with severe haemophilia (FVIII <1%) – it will not raise FVIII enough to stop bleeding.
- Do not perform a lumbar puncture or joint aspiration without factor cover (target FVIII >50% for safe procedure).
- Do not prescribe NSAIDs (including topical) for pain – risk of catastrophic bleed.
- Do not assume a baby is unaffected because of no family history – check cord blood FVIII in males from known carriers, or do genetic testing.
- Do not forget to check FVIII trough levels before discharge – a low trough predicts rebleeding.

---

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | **Haemophilia A** | **Haemophilia B (Christmas Disease)** | **von Willebrand Disease (vWD)** | **Acquired Haemophilia A** |
|---------|-------------------|---------------------------------------|----------------------------------|-----------------------------|
| **Inheritance** | X-linked recessive | X-linked recessive | Autosomal dominant (most) | Acquired (autoimmune) |
| **Deficiency** | Factor VIII | Factor IX | von Willebrand factor (with secondary FVIII drop) | Autoantibody to Factor VIII |
| **Bleeding Pattern** | Deep joint bleeds (knee, ankle, elbow), muscle haematomas | Same as Haemophilia A | Mucocutaneous (epistaxis, menorrhagia, gum bleeding), but can have post-surgical bleeding; rarely haemarthrosis | Skin ecchymoses, soft tissue haematomas, retroperitoneal and intracranial bleeds; **joint bleeds are rare** |
| **Sex** | Almost always male | Almost always male | Male and female equally | Male or female; often older adults or postpartum |
| **Family History** | Often positive (maternal uncle, brother) | Often positive | Often positive (autosomal) | **Negative** – new onset in adult |
| **Screening Labs** | aPTT ↑, PT normal, mixing corrects | aPTT ↑, PT normal, mixing corrects | aPTT ↑ (if FVIII low), PT normal, bleeding time ↑, PFA-100 ↑ | aPTT ↑↑, mixing **does not correct** |
| **Specific Lab Confirmation** | FVIII activity ↓, VWF:Ag normal | FIX activity ↓ | VWF:Ag ↓, ristocetin cofactor activity ↓, FVIII activity may be ↓ | Bethesda assay positive, FVIII activity ↓, mixing fails |
| **Key Differentiator** | Low FVIII with normal VWF – pattern of joint bleeds | Low FIX – same pattern but factor IX (heparin-binding) | Mucocutaneous bleeding + low VWF; bleeding time prolonged | No family history, adult, **mixing study fails**, bleeding in soft tissues not joints |

**Pattern cue:** If a child presents with a haemarthrosis and prolonged aPTT, the two X-linked disorders (A and B) are top. Differentiate by specific factor assays. If an adult presents with new-onset easy bruising and aPTT not correcting, think acquired haemophilia A.

---

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Complication | How It Presents | Key Signs | What To Do |
|--------------|----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Chronic Haemophilic Arthropathy** | Repeated haemarthroses → synovitis → cartilage loss → joint contractures and ankylosis. Pain, decreased range of motion, deformity. | Warm, boggy knee; flexion contracture; MRI shows synovial hypertrophy and bone erosions. | Early prophylaxis prevents it. Established arthropathy: physiotherapy, replacement joints (tolerated well with factor cover). |
| **Intracranial Haemorrhage** | Headache, vomiting, altered consciousness, focal neurological deficit, seizures. Often after minor head trauma. | GCS drop, papilloedema, unilateral weakness. CT shows subdural, intraparenchymal, or subarachnoid blood. | **Emergency:** Administer FVIII 100 IU/kg IV stat, call haematology, neurosurgeon, ICU. Repeat factor to maintain trough >80% for 7-10 days. |
| **Iliopsoas Bleed (Compartment Syndrome)** | Groin or lower back pain, hip held in flexion, unable to extend thigh. Femoral nerve compression → anterior thigh numbness, quadriceps weakness. | Positive psoas sign (pain on hip extension), reduced sensation over anterior thigh, weak knee extension. | FVIII 50 IU/kg, bedrest, avoid flexed position. If compartment syndrome confirmed (compartment pressure >30 mmHg), urgent fasciotomy. |
| **Inhibitor Development** | Patient's bleeds no longer respond to standard FVIII doses; aPTT remains prolonged despite FVIII infusion. | Mixing study fails to correct, Bethesda titre >0.5. | For acute bleeds: bypassing agents (rFVIIa 90 mcg/kg every 2-3 hours; or aPCC 50 U/kg 12-hourly). Eradicate inhibitor with ITI. |
| **Transfusion-Transmitted Infections (historical)** | HCV (hepatitis C), HIV (now rare with recombinant products). | Abnormal LFTs, jaundice, HIV seroconversion. | Screen all haemophilia patients for HCV and HIV who received plasma-derived concentrates before 1990s. |

**Late-Presentation Pattern (What If Untreated/Unrecognised?):**
- A 10-year-old boy from a region with limited access to healthcare presents with bilateral knee contractures, severe pain, inability to walk. He has had multiple transient knee swellings since toddlerhood, each treated with rest and/or folk remedies. Family history: several maternal uncles died young from "bleeding fits." This is end-stage haemophilic arthropathy – irreversible joint destruction. Lifesaving intervention: factor replacement, orthopaedic referral for joint replacement, and lifelong prophylaxis to prevent further bleeds.

---

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonic: "HAEMOPHILIA"**  
- **H** – Hemarthrosis (joint bleeding – hallmark)  
- **A** – aPTT prolonged  
- **E** – X-linked (E for "X")  
- **M** – Mixing study corrects (factor deficiency)  
- **O
